title: Remove adjacent duplicates
description: Remove all adjacent duplicates in a string recursively

examples.explanation: Start: 'abbaca'. Pass 1: Scan: 'a' vs 'b' → no, keep 'a'; 'b' vs 'b' → duplicate, skip both; 'a' vs 'c' → no, keep 'a'; 'c' vs 'a' → no, keep 'c'; 'a' → keep 'a'. New string: 'aaca'. Pass 2: Scan 'aaca': 'a' vs 'a' → duplicate, skip both; 'c' vs 'a' → no, keep 'c'; 'a' → keep 'a'. New string: 'ca'. Pass 3: Scan 'ca': 'c' vs 'a' → no, keep 'c'; 'a' → keep 'a'. No duplicates found. Return 'ca'.

Start: 'azxxzy'. Pass 1: Scan: 'a' vs 'z' → no, keep 'a'; 'z' vs 'x' → no, keep 'z'; 'x' vs 'x' → duplicate, skip both; 'z' vs 'y' → no, keep 'z'; 'y' → keep 'y'. New string: 'azzy'. Pass 2: Scan 'azzy': 'a' vs 'z' → no, keep 'a'; 'z' vs 'z' → duplicate, skip both; 'y' → keep 'y'. New string: 'ay'. Pass 3: Scan 'ay': 'a' vs 'y' → no, keep 'a'; 'y' → keep 'y'. No duplicates found. Return 'ay'.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iteratively scan the string to find and remove adjacent duplicates, repeating until no duplicates remain. This is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated passes over the string.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove All Adjacent Duplicates Recursively Using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation that repeatedly scans the string to find and remove adjacent duplicates until none remain.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach tackles the problem by repeatedly scanning the string from left to right, identifying adjacent duplicate characters, removing them, and then repeating the process on the modified string until no more adjacent duplicates exist. The intuition is that removing duplicates might create new adjacent duplicates (e.g., in 'abba', removing 'bb' creates 'aa' which are adjacent), so we need to keep scanning until the string stabilizes. The algorithm works by using a while loop that continues as long as we find duplicates. In each iteration, we traverse the string, comparing each character with its next neighbor. When we find a duplicate pair, we remove both characters by building a new string without them. This process repeats until a full pass finds no duplicates. The time complexity is O(n²) in the worst case because we might make O(n) passes (e.g., 'abc...cba' pattern) and each pass takes O(n) time for scanning and building strings. Space complexity is O(n) for storing intermediate strings. This connects to L1 as L0 uses the duplicate removal subroutine from L1 within a loop. Common mistakes include not handling the string modification correctly during iteration (which can cause index errors) and not continuing the loop after removal (missing newly formed duplicates). Real code pattern: def removeDuplicates(s): while True: new_s = ''; i = 0; found = False; while i < len(s): if i+1 < len(s) and s[i] == s[i+1]: found = True; i += 2; else: new_s += s[i]; i += 1; if not found: return new_s; s = new_s.

examples.trace: Start: 'abbaca'. Pass 1: Scan: 'a' vs 'b' → no, keep 'a'; 'b' vs 'b' → duplicate, skip both; 'a' vs 'c' → no, keep 'a'; 'c' vs 'a' → no, keep 'c'; 'a' → keep 'a'. New string: 'aaca'. Pass 2: Scan 'aaca': 'a' vs 'a' → duplicate, skip both; 'c' vs 'a' → no, keep 'c'; 'a' → keep 'a'. New string: 'ca'. Pass 3: Scan 'ca': 'c' vs 'a' → no, keep 'c'; 'a' → keep 'a'. No duplicates found. Return 'ca'.

Start: 'azxxzy'. Pass 1: Scan: 'a' vs 'z' → no, keep 'a'; 'z' vs 'x' → no, keep 'z'; 'x' vs 'x' → duplicate, skip both; 'z' vs 'y' → no, keep 'z'; 'y' → keep 'y'. New string: 'azzy'. Pass 2: Scan 'azzy': 'a' vs 'z' → no, keep 'a'; 'z' vs 'z' → duplicate, skip both; 'y' → keep 'y'. New string: 'ay'. Pass 3: Scan 'ay': 'a' vs 'y' → no, keep 'a'; 'y' → keep 'y'. No duplicates found. Return 'ay'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Single Pass Adjacent Duplicate Removal
ladders.desc: Scan a string once to remove all adjacent duplicate pairs, returning the modified string.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs a single left-to-right scan of the input string to identify and remove adjacent duplicate characters. The intuition is that by traversing the string and comparing each character with its immediate neighbor, we can detect pairs where s[i] == s[i+1] and skip both characters in the output. We build a new string by iterating through indices: if we find a duplicate pair at i and i+1, we increment i by 2 to skip both; otherwise, we add s[i] to the new string and increment i by 1. This ensures that all adjacent duplicates found in this pass are removed, but note that new duplicates might form after removal (e.g., removing 'bb' from 'abba' leaves 'aa', which are now adjacent but not removed in this pass). The time complexity is O(n) for the single scan, and space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This connects to L0 as L0 calls this subroutine repeatedly until no duplicates are found. Common mistakes include incorrect index handling when skipping duplicates (e.g., not incrementing properly) or not checking bounds when accessing s[i+1]. Real code pattern: def removeDuplicatesSinglePass(s): new_s = ''; i = 0; while i < len(s): if i+1 < len(s) and s[i] == s[i+1]: i += 2; else: new_s += s[i]; i += 1; return new_s.

examples.trace: Input: 'abbaca'. Start i=0: s[0]='a', s[1]='b' → not equal, add 'a' to new_s, i=1. i=1: s[1]='b', s[2]='b' → equal, skip both, i=3. i=3: s[3]='a', s[4]='c' → not equal, add 'a', i=4. i=4: s[4]='c', s[5]='a' → not equal, add 'c', i=5. i=5: s[5]='a', no s[6], add 'a', i=6. End. new_s = 'aaca'.

Input: 'azxxzy'. Start i=0: s[0]='a', s[1]='z' → not equal, add 'a', i=1. i=1: s[1]='z', s[2]='x' → not equal, add 'z', i=2. i=2: s[2]='x', s[3]='x' → equal, skip both, i=4. i=4: s[4]='z', s[5]='y' → not equal, add 'z', i=5. i=5: s[5]='y', no s[6], add 'y', i=6. End. new_s = 'azzy'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative String Modification with Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: The technique of repeatedly modifying a string based on a condition (adjacent equality) until a stable state is reached.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the idea of applying an operation (removing adjacent duplicates) to a string iteratively until no further changes occur. The intuition is that some problems require multiple passes because an operation can create new instances of the condition being checked. Here, after removing one pair of duplicates, new characters might become adjacent and also be duplicates, so we must re-scan. This is a form of fixed-point iteration: we keep applying the transformation until the output equals the input. The algorithm uses a while loop that checks if a pass found any duplicates; if yes, it updates the string and repeats; if no, it returns the string. The key insight is that we need a flag to track whether any duplicates were removed in a pass. Time complexity depends on the number of passes, which can be O(n) in worst case, making overall O(n²). Space complexity is O(n) for temporary strings. This connects to L1 as L2 uses the single-pass removal from L1 inside the loop. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the string correctly or missing the flag reset. Real code pattern: def removeDuplicatesIterative(s): while True: new_s = ''; i = 0; found = False; while i < len(s): if i+1 < len(s) and s[i] == s[i+1]: found = True; i += 2; else: new_s += s[i]; i += 1; if not found: return new_s; s = new_s.

examples.trace: Start: 'abba'. Pass 1: Single pass removes 'bb' → new string 'aa'. found=True. Update s='aa'. Pass 2: Single pass on 'aa' removes 'aa' → new string ''. found=True. Update s=''. Pass 3: Single pass on '' finds no duplicates, found=False. Return ''.

Start: 'abcddcba'. Pass 1: Remove 'dd' → 'abccba'. found=True. s='abccba'. Pass 2: Remove 'cc' → 'abba'. found=True. s='abba'. Pass 3: Remove 'bb' → 'aa'. found=True. s='aa'. Pass 4: Remove 'aa' → ''. found=True. s=''. Pass 5: No duplicates, found=False. Return ''.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Traversal and Comparison
ladders.desc: Scanning a string to compare each character with its neighbor and collect non-duplicates into a new string.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a string from left to right, comparing each character with the next one, and building a new string that excludes characters when a duplicate pair is found. The intuition is that by iterating through indices, we can examine pairs (s[i], s[i+1]) and decide whether to include s[i] in the output based on the comparison. If they are equal, we skip both; otherwise, we include s[i] and move on. At the end, if i points to the last character (no next neighbor), we include it. This requires careful index management to avoid out-of-bounds errors. The time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, and space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This connects to L2 as L3 is the fundamental operation used within each pass of the iterative process. Common mistakes include not handling the last character separately or incorrectly updating indices when skipping duplicates. Real code pattern: def traverseAndCompare(s): result = ''; i = 0; while i < len(s): if i+1 < len(s) and s[i] == s[i+1]: i += 2; else: result += s[i]; i += 1; return result.

examples.trace: Input: 'aabb'. Start i=0: s[0]='a', s[1]='a' → equal, skip both, i=2. i=2: s[2]='b', s[3]='b' → equal, skip both, i=4. End. result = ''.

Input: 'abcde'. Start i=0: s[0]='a', s[1]='b' → not equal, add 'a', i=1. i=1: s[1]='b', s[2]='c' → not equal, add 'b', i=2. i=2: s[2]='c', s[3]='d' → not equal, add 'c', i=3. i=3: s[3]='d', s[4]='e' → not equal, add 'd', i=4. i=4: s[4]='e', no s[5], add 'e', i=5. End. result = 'abcde'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and String Building
ladders.desc: Comparing two characters for equality and constructing a new string by appending characters.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental actions: comparing if two characters are equal, and building a string by concatenating characters. The intuition is that in many string manipulation problems, we need to check conditions between characters (like adjacency) and then create a modified string based on those checks. Character comparison is straightforward using the equality operator (==). String building can be done by initializing an empty string and using the += operator to append characters one by one. This is efficient in terms of clarity, though for large strings, using a list and joining might be better. Time complexity for building a string of length n by appending is O(n²) in some languages due to immutability, but in Python-style pseudocode, we assume O(n) per operation for simplicity. Space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This connects to L3 as L4 provides the tools needed for the traversal and comparison in L3. Common mistakes include using incorrect comparison operators or not initializing the string properly. Real code pattern: def basicOps(s): result = ''; for i in range(len(s)-1): if s[i] == s[i+1]: # comparison; else: result += s[i] # building; if len(s) > 0: result += s[-1]; return result.

examples.trace: Input: 'ab'. Initialize result=''. Compare s[0]='a' and s[1]='b': not equal, so result += 'a' → result='a'. End of loop. Add last character s[1]='b': result += 'b' → result='ab'. Return 'ab'.

Input: 'aa'. Initialize result=''. Compare s[0]='a' and s[1]='a': equal, so do nothing. End of loop. Add last character s[1]='a': but since we skipped both, we might not add; in this pattern, we add s[-1] only if not skipped, so result remains ''. Return ''.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Looping
ladders.desc: Accessing characters in a string by index and iterating through them using a loop.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers how to work with strings as sequences of characters, where each character can be accessed using an index (starting from 0). The intuition is that strings are like arrays of characters, and we can use loops to traverse each character one by one. For example, in Python-style pseudocode, s[i] gives the character at position i, and len(s) gives the total number of characters. A common loop structure is a while loop with an index variable that increments until it reaches len(s). This allows us to examine each character and perform operations based on its value or position. Time complexity for traversing a string of length n is O(n), and space complexity is O(1) if no extra storage is used. This connects to L4 as L5 provides the basic mechanism to access characters for comparison in L4. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., accessing s[len(s)] which is out of bounds) or not updating the index correctly. Real code pattern: def stringTraversal(s): i = 0; while i < len(s): char = s[i]; # do something with char; i += 1.

examples.output: Characters: h e l l o
examples.trace: Input: 'hello'. Start i=0: s[0]='h', print or store 'h', i=1. i=1: s[1]='e', process 'e', i=2. i=2: s[2]='l', process 'l', i=3. i=3: s[3]='l', process 'l', i=4. i=4: s[4]='o', process 'o', i=5. i=5 equals len(s)=5, loop ends.
Input: 'abc'. Start i=0: s[0]='a', process 'a', i=1. i=1: s[1]='b', process 'b', i=2. i=2: s[2]='c', process 'c', i=3. i=3 equals len(s)=3, loop ends.

testCases.expected: Characters: h e l l o
Characters: i n d e x
Characters: s t r i n g

approaches.name: Stack-Based Linear O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to traverse the string once, pushing characters and popping when adjacent duplicates are found, then reconstruct the result. This avoids recursion overhead while maintaining linear time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Adjacent Duplicates Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of removing all adjacent duplicates from a string using a stack-based linear approach.
ladders.explanation: The stack-based approach to removing adjacent duplicates works by processing the string character by character while maintaining a stack that represents the result string without adjacent duplicates. The intuition is that a stack naturally handles the 'adjacent' requirement through its LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) property - when we encounter a character, we can easily check if it matches the top of the stack (the most recent character). If it matches, we've found adjacent duplicates and pop from the stack. Otherwise, we push the character onto the stack. This approach is linear because we process each character exactly once, and the stack operations are O(1). The space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when there are no duplicates. The algorithm begins by initializing an empty stack. We iterate through each character in the input string. For each character, we check if the stack is not empty AND the current character equals the top of the stack. If true, we pop from the stack (removing the duplicate). If false, we push the character onto the stack. After processing all characters, we convert the stack to a string by joining from bottom to top. This gives us the result with all adjacent duplicates removed. The key insight is that the stack maintains the invariant that it never contains adjacent duplicates at any point during processing. This approach avoids recursion overhead while maintaining O(n) time complexity. Common mistakes include forgetting to check if the stack is empty before accessing the top, incorrectly reconstructing the result string (stack order matters), and not handling edge cases like empty strings or strings with all duplicates. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete stack processing logic described in the key subroutine.

examples.trace:
Input: 'abbaca'
Initialize stack = []
Process 'a': stack empty → push 'a' → stack = ['a']
Process 'b': top='a', current='b' → different → push 'b' → stack = ['a', 'b']
Process 'b': top='b', current='b' → same → pop 'b' → stack = ['a']
Process 'a': top='a', current='a' → same → pop 'a' → stack = []
Process 'c': stack empty → push 'c' → stack = ['c']
Process 'a': top='c', current='a' → different → push 'a' → stack = ['c', 'a']
Convert stack to string: 'ca'
Output: 'ca'

Input: 'azxxzy'
Initialize stack = []
Process 'a': stack empty → push 'a' → stack = ['a']
Process 'z': top='a', current='z' → different → push 'z' → stack = ['a', 'z']
Process 'x': top='z', current='x' → different → push 'x' → stack = ['a', 'z', 'x']
Process 'x': top='x', current='x' → same → pop 'x' → stack = ['a', 'z']
Process 'z': top='z', current='z' → same → pop 'z' → stack = ['a']
Process 'y': top='a', current='y' → different → push 'y' → stack = ['a', 'y']
Convert stack to string: 'ay'
Output: 'ay'

ladders.title: Key Subroutine — Stack Processing for Adjacent Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Implement the core stack processing logic that detects and removes adjacent duplicates during traversal.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine implements the heart of the stack-based approach: processing characters one by one while maintaining a stack that never contains adjacent duplicates. The intuition is that by using a stack's LIFO property, we can efficiently check for adjacency - the top of the stack always represents the most recently added character that hasn't been removed due to duplicates. When we encounter a new character, we compare it with the top of the stack. If they match, we've found adjacent duplicates (since the top character was the previous character in the original string), so we pop to remove both. If they don't match, we push the new character. This process ensures the stack invariant: at any point, the stack contains a prefix of the processed string with all adjacent duplicates removed. The algorithm takes an input string and returns the stack after processing. We initialize an empty stack, then iterate through each character. For each character c: if stack is not empty AND c equals stack[-1], then pop(); else push(c). After the loop, return the stack. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as each character causes at most one push and one pop. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. This subroutine connects to L0 as it forms the main processing loop of the complete solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in iteration, incorrect comparison logic (forgetting to check stack emptiness first), and misunderstanding that we're comparing with the most recent kept character, not the original adjacent character. The real code pattern uses simple stack operations with careful condition checking.

examples.trace:
Input: 'abb'
Initialize stack = []
Process 'a': stack empty → push 'a' → stack = ['a']
Process 'b': top='a', current='b' → different → push 'b' → stack = ['a', 'b']
Process 'b': top='b', current='b' → same → pop 'b' → stack = ['a']
Return stack: ['a']

Input: 'abba'
Initialize stack = []
Process 'a': stack empty → push 'a' → stack = ['a']
Process 'b': top='a', current='b' → different → push 'b' → stack = ['a', 'b']
Process 'b': top='b', current='b' → same → pop 'b' → stack = ['a']
Process 'a': top='a', current='a' → same → pop 'a' → stack = []
Return stack: []

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using LIFO Property for Adjacency Checking
ladders.desc: Understand how stack's Last-In-First-Out property enables efficient adjacent duplicate detection.
ladders.explanation: The core logic leverages the stack's LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) property to solve the adjacent duplicate problem efficiently. The intuition is that adjacency in a string is about the relationship between consecutive characters. A stack naturally captures this relationship because when we process characters sequentially, the top of the stack always represents the most recent character that hasn't been removed. Therefore, checking if the current character equals the top of the stack is exactly checking for adjacency in the original string. When we find a match, we pop the stack, effectively removing both adjacent duplicates. This works because the character at the top was added from the immediately preceding position (unless it was also part of a previous removal chain). The step-by-step reasoning: 1) Process string left to right. 2) Maintain stack representing processed portion without adjacent duplicates. 3) For each new character, compare with stack top. 4) If equal, pop (remove both). 5) If not equal, push (keep character). This ensures the stack never has adjacent duplicates. The time complexity for this logic is O(1) per character comparison, leading to O(n) overall. Space is O(n) for the stack. This connects to L1 as it explains why the stack processing algorithm works. Common mistakes include misunderstanding that we're comparing with the actual adjacent character in the original string (which the stack top correctly represents) and not realizing that multiple pops might be needed in recursive removal scenarios (though our approach handles this through sequential processing). The real pattern is simple: use stack[-1] to access most recent kept character and compare with current.

examples.trace:
Scenario: Processing 'b' when stack contains ['a']
Stack top = 'a' (most recent kept character)
Current character = 'b'
Comparison: 'a' != 'b'
Since they're different, 'b' is not adjacent duplicate of 'a'
Action: Push 'b' onto stack
New stack: ['a', 'b']
Explanation: 'b' is kept because it's not duplicate of previous kept character 'a'

Scenario: Processing 'b' when stack contains ['a', 'b']
Stack top = 'b' (most recent kept character)
Current character = 'b'
Comparison: 'b' == 'b'
Since they're equal, we found adjacent duplicates
The 'b' at stack top came from immediately previous position
Current 'b' is adjacent duplicate of that previous 'b'
Action: Pop 'b' from stack
New stack: ['a']
Explanation: Both 'b' characters are removed as adjacent duplicates

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Character Management
ladders.desc: Implement basic stack operations (push, pop, peek) to manage characters during string processing.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on implementing and using fundamental stack operations to manage characters as we process the string. A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) principle, which makes it perfect for our adjacent duplicate problem because we only need to access the most recently added item. The three essential operations are: 1) push(item): adds an item to the top of the stack. 2) pop(): removes and returns the top item from the stack. 3) peek() or top(): returns the top item without removing it (often implemented as stack[-1] in Python). For our problem, we'll use a list to implement the stack. The intuition is that we need to frequently check what the last kept character was (peek), add new characters when they're not duplicates (push), and remove characters when we find duplicates (pop). The algorithm: Initialize empty list as stack. When processing a character: First check if stack is not empty using len(stack) > 0. If not empty, use stack[-1] to peek at top element. Compare with current character. If equal, call stack.pop() to remove the top. If not equal, call stack.append(current) to push. Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the stack. This connects to L2 as these operations enable the LIFO-based adjacency checking. Common mistakes include trying to peek when stack is empty (causing index error), using wrong list methods (append for push, pop for pop), and misunderstanding that pop() removes from end in Python lists. The real pattern uses list append for push, list pop for pop, and list[-1] for peek with proper empty check.

examples.trace:
Initial stack: ['a', 'b']
Operation: push('c')
Using list append: stack.append('c')
Append adds 'c' to the end of the list
End of list is top of stack
New stack: ['a', 'b', 'c']
Top is now 'c'
Time: O(1)

Initial stack: ['x', 'y', 'z']
Operation: pop()
Using list pop: stack.pop()
pop() removes and returns last element 'z'
New stack: ['x', 'y']
Top is now 'y'
Time: O(1)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Adjacent Characters in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Compare each character with its immediate neighbor in a string to identify adjacent duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation focuses on the fundamental task of comparing each character in a string with its immediate neighbor to find adjacent duplicates. The intuition is simple: two characters are adjacent duplicates if they are the same character and appear consecutively in the string. For example, in 'hello', 'l' and 'l' are adjacent duplicates at positions 2 and 3 (0-indexed). The algorithm involves iterating through the string and comparing each character with the next one. We can use a loop that goes from index 0 to len(string)-2. At each position i, compare string[i] with string[i+1]. If they are equal, we've found adjacent duplicates. The time complexity is O(n) for scanning the string once. Space complexity is O(1) if we just count or mark duplicates, or O(n) if we build a new string. This operation connects to L3 because in the stack approach, we're effectively doing this comparison but using the stack top instead of the actual next character (which works due to the removal of intermediate duplicates). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries (going beyond string length), confusing adjacent with any duplicates (adjacent means immediately next to each other), and not handling empty or single-character strings. The real pattern uses a simple for loop with range(len(s)-1) and comparison using == operator.

examples.trace:
String: 'abccba'
Indices: 0:a, 1:b, 2:c, 3:c, 4:b, 5:a
Compare index 0-1: 'a' vs 'b' → different
Compare index 1-2: 'b' vs 'c' → different
Compare index 2-3: 'c' vs 'c' → SAME (adjacent duplicates at positions 2,3)
Compare index 3-4: 'c' vs 'b' → different
Compare index 4-5: 'b' vs 'a' → different
Found one pair of adjacent duplicates: 'cc' at positions 2,3

String: 'aabb'
Indices: 0:a, 1:a, 2:b, 3:b
Compare index 0-1: 'a' vs 'a' → SAME (adjacent duplicates at positions 0,1)
Compare index 1-2: 'a' vs 'b' → different
Compare index 2-3: 'b' vs 'b' → SAME (adjacent duplicates at positions 2,3)
Found two pairs of adjacent duplicates: 'aa' and 'bb'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Character Access
ladders.desc: Learn basic string operations: accessing characters by index and iterating through strings.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the most basic prerequisite: understanding strings as sequences of characters and how to access individual characters. A string is an ordered collection of characters, like 'hello' which contains characters 'h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o' in that order. Each character has a position called an index, starting from 0. So in 'hello', index 0 is 'h', index 1 is 'e', index 2 is 'l', index 3 is 'l', index 4 is 'o'. The intuition is that to work with adjacent duplicates, we need to be able to look at individual characters and their positions. The basic operations are: 1) Access character at specific index using s[i] notation. 2) Get string length using len(s). 3) Iterate through all characters using a for loop. For example, for char in s: processes each character. Or for i in range(len(s)): gives indices to access s[i]. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for accessing, O(n) if creating new strings. This connects to L4 because comparing adjacent characters requires accessing characters at specific positions. Common mistakes include index out of range errors (trying to access s[len(s)]), confusing 0-indexing with 1-indexing, and not understanding that strings are immutable in many languages. The real pattern uses simple indexing and loops to traverse strings.

examples.trace:
String: 'cat'
Length: len('cat') = 3
Indices: 0:'c', 1:'a', 2:'t'
Access index 1: s[1] = 'a'
Explanation: Index 1 refers to the second character (since starting from 0)
Valid indices are 0, 1, 2
Index 1 is within range 0 to 2

String: 'dog'
Method 1: for char in 'dog':
  First iteration: char = 'd'
  Second iteration: char = 'o'
  Third iteration: char = 'g'
Method 2: for i in range(len('dog')):  # range(3) gives 0,1,2
  i=0: 'dog'[0] = 'd'
  i=1: 'dog'[1] = 'o'
  i=2: 'dog'[2] = 'g'
Both methods access all characters in order
